Keta Lagoon Complex \- Four Priority Areas of Interest \(AoIs\)

DJI Mavic 3M Survey | EOSDA Satellite Platform | Community Monitoring Integration

Survey Coverage

1,467 Ha across 4 Areas of Interest, Keta Lagoon Complex

Satellite Platform

EOSDA Crop Monitoring \(Sentinel\-2, 10 m, 3–5\-day revisit\)

Survey Period

February–March 2026

Report Date

March 2026

Report Version

V2\.0 \- Standalone Management Report

__Chapter 1__

__Introduction — Scope, Mandate, and Report Architecture__

# Chapter 1: Introduction and Mandate

## 1\.1 The Case for High\-Resolution Monitoring at Scale

Following the establishment of the mangrove extent baseline of Ghana, it became obvious that the resources should be channeled to the Keta Lagoon Complex Ramsar Site\.  The Ramsar Site contains 20,717\.95 hectares of mangroves, approximately 69 percent of Ghana's entire coastal mangrove resource concentrated in a single ecosystem\. At a historical deforestation rate of 2\.15 percent per year, the Lagoon is losing approximately 445 hectares of this resource annually to fuelwood harvesting, sediment starvation following the impoundment of the Volta River at Akosombo, and the encroachment of agricultural and aquacultural activity into marginal mangrove stands\. The scale and pace of that loss defines the monitoring challenge this report addresses\.

Conventional satellite imagery at 10 to 30 metre resolution is the standard instrument for national\-scale mangrove monitoring, and for broad coverage it is adequate\. Sentinel\-2 imagery can detect large\-scale clearance events, track decadal changes in ecosystem extent, and generate the annual cover maps that feed into national greenhouse gas inventories\. What it cannot do is consequential for a carbon project\. At 10 metres, a satellite pixel cannot reliably distinguish *Rhizophora mangle* from *Avicennia germinans* canopy, species whose above\-ground biomass differs significantly and whose distinction matters directly to carbon stock estimates\. It cannot resolve the micro\-topographic drainage channels that determine whether a degraded area qualifies as a hydrologically feasible restoration zone\. Most critically for carbon accounting, the early\-stage thinning and die\-back that precede visible deforestation by months or years, representing the leading edge of the annual loss, fall below the detection threshold of any freely available satellite product\.

These are not merely technical inconveniences\. In a carbon project, undetected deforestation that precedes a measurement cycle can invalidate carbon stock claims and trigger verification failures\. A monitoring system that relies exclusively on satellite imagery will systematically underestimate loss during periods of incremental degradation, precisely when early intervention would be most effective and least costly\. The drone survey and EOSDA continuous monitoring system described in this report fills that detection gap\. Centimetre\-scale orthomosaic imagery across the four priority Areas of Interest, combined with a recurring Sentinel\-2 workflow applying NDVI and NDMI\-based change detection on a three\-to\-five\-day revisit cycle, provides the multi\-resolution detection architecture that a credible, verifiable blue carbon project requires\. This system does not replace the national satellite baseline; it anchors it with the spatial precision that carbon verifiers and ecological managers cannot obtain from orbit\.

## 1\.2 Terms of Reference Mandate

Phase 3, the Generation of a National Blue Carbon Ecosystem Cover Map, requires that drone shots or aerial photographs documenting the current state of priority areas be assembled as a collage and stored in a project database\. This requirement recognises that satellite\-derived land cover classification must be grounded by direct aerial observation: orthomosaics provide a permanent, georeferenced visual record of ecosystem condition at the baseline date that an independent verifier can interrogate directly, and that satellite analysis alone cannot supply\.

Phase 4, the Blue Carbon Inventory, requires that the boundaries of the assessment area be precisely defined and well\-documented, to serve as the baseline for all subsequent estimates of total carbon stock or carbon stock change\. The four AoI boundary files that anchor this report, produced between February and March 2026 and archived as georeferenced GeoJSON and KML datasets, are the direct delivery of this Phase 4 obligation\. They are the spatial foundation of the carbon accounting chain, and every future stock estimate for the Keta Lagoon pilot traces back to the boundaries documented here\.

Taken together, Phases 3 and 4 specify four deliverables for this workstream: baseline orthomosaics at centimetre\-scale ground sample distance for each priority AoI; authoritative boundary datasets in georeferenced vector format; EOSDA platform configuration for recurring Sentinel\-2 observation; and a handover documentation package enabling MEST and MLNR staff to manage the continuous monitoring workflow independently of the consulting team\. This report provides a transparent technical record of progress against each deliverable, with honest accounting of what has been completed and what remains to be operationalised\.

## 1\.3 Four Areas of Interest: The Gold Standard Monitoring Units

The four Areas of Interest that constitute the primary monitoring footprint of this workstream are not an arbitrary selection from the Keta landscape\. They represent the spatial intersection of three simultaneous requirements: the zones of highest ecological significance within the Lagoon, as measured by carbon stock density and restoration potential; the zones most accessible to the community monitoring network that provides the ground\-truth layer; and the zones where aerial observation and ground\-level monitoring together create the strongest multi\-scale detection architecture\.

Aborlove Nolopi \(52 Ha, eastern cluster\) covers a mixed landscape of degraded and intact mangrove where the boundary between recoverable and non\-recoverable canopy is precisely the question that centimetre\-resolution orthomosaics can resolve and satellite imagery cannot\. Agbatsivi\-Agortoe \(762 Ha, central cluster\), the largest of the four AoIs, spans contiguous and fragmented canopy across a landscape subject to sustained fuelwood pressure from fish\-smoking operations\. Salo\-Agortoe Stretch \(525 Ha, central cluster\) encompasses the creek system most accessible to the Salo community monitoring network and hosts Salo Temporary Plots\. Anyanui Combine \(128 Ha, western cluster\) is a restoration potential zone, the AoI most suited to prospective carbon sequestration accounting once drone\-derived topographic data confirms hydrological feasibility for replanting\.

Together these four AoIs total 1,467 hectares, strategically selected fraction of the 20,717\.95\-hectare Lagoon that overlaps directly with the ecosystem health monitoring clusters described in the companion Wetland Ecosystem Health Report\. That overlap is intentional architecture\. Where the 57\-station ground monitoring network measures water quality, soil chemistry, and vegetation condition at fixed points within these zones, the drone orthomosaics provide the spatial context that gives those point measurements landscape\-level interpretive power\. The AoIs are where the monitoring system is most integrated and where the evidence base for carbon verification is strongest\.

## 1\.4 The FPIC Dimension: Why Social Licence Precedes Technical Operations

Before any drone overflies community land and before any monitoring boundary is registered in a continuous monitoring platform, the communities whose land and livelihoods fall within or adjacent to the AoIs must be genuinely informed and their consent freely given\. This is not a procedural courtesy, it is a binding obligation under the World Bank's Environmental and Social Framework, specifically under ESS7 \(engagement with historically underserved traditional and local communities\) and ESS10 \(meaningful stakeholder engagement and information disclosure\)\. Violation of these standards does not merely compromise a process requirement; it can trigger project suspension and invalidate the carbon credits the monitoring system is designed to support\.

The stakes of Free, Prior, and Informed Consent are elevated here because this is a carbon monitoring programme with direct financial implications for the communities within its boundaries\. The orthomosaic data, boundary files, and continuous monitoring outputs generated by this system will be used to support carbon credit claims under Verra VM0033 and related methodologies\. Carbon credits have a market value\. The communities of Aborlove, Agbatsivi\-Agortoe, Salo, and Anyanui have a right to understand that monitoring of their land generates commercially significant data, to participate in governance decisions about how that data is held and disclosed, and to receive a defined and equitable share of the financial outcomes their landscapes enable\. FPIC combined with data access agreements and benefit\-sharing arrangements, is a prerequisite for the legitimacy of the carbon accounting chain, not an optional prelude to it\. Chapter 3 of this report documents the FPIC engagement process for each AoI cluster: consent status, the institutional roles of Community Change Agents and CREMA structures in sustaining community governance, and the outstanding steps required before drone survey outputs can be formally disclosed or used in any carbon market application\.

## 1\.5 Structure of This Report

This report is organised in six chapters\. Chapter 2 establishes the strategic and ecological context of the Keta Lagoon Complex, providing the biophysical rationale for AoI selection\. Chapter 3 documents the FPIC process: engagement history, consent status by cluster, data access arrangements, and the governance architecture ensuring communities are participants in the monitoring system rather than merely its subjects\. Chapter 4 covers drone survey methodology, drone, sensors, flight planning, ground control points, orthomosaic processing, and quality assurance\. Chapter 5 presents the spatial outputs: AoI boundary datasets, orthomosaic products, and condition mapping\. The sixth chapter details the ingestion of the four AoIs in EOSDA Monitoring platform and the analysis that could be done using the 3\-5 days data cycle\.

## 1\.6 Relationship to the Four\-Report Architecture

The Drone Survey and EOSDA Continuous Monitoring Report is the remote sensing and spatial precision layer of the Ghana Blue Carbon Assessment\. It derives its full analytical power from integration with three companion reports that together constitute the four\-report assessment package delivered under WACA ResIP 2\.

The Wetland Ecosystem Health Report provides the ground\-truth layer; water quality, soil chemistry, and vegetation condition measured at 65 stations within and adjacent to the Keta Lagoon\. Orthomosaics from the drone survey supply the spatial context within which those point measurements can be interpreted at landscape scale: the Ecosystem Health Report documents what is happening at specific locations; this report provides the aerial view that situates those locations within the canopy structure and condition of the broader AoI\.

The Carbon Inventory and Stock Assessment Report is the primary downstream consumer of this report's spatial products\. Orthomosaic outputs support biomass stratification by canopy type and condition class, enabling allometric modelling of above\-ground carbon stocks with a spatial precision that satellite imagery alone cannot provide\. The Salo Temporary Sample Plots boundary is the direct spatial bridge between the inventory's field measurements and the AoI monitoring framework\.

The National Blue Carbon MRV Framework Report specifies how all monitoring outputs, i\.e drone\-derived spatial products and EOSDA alert signals alike, feed into Ghana's national carbon accounting cycle, fulfil Nationally Determined Contribution reporting obligations, and meet the verification requirements of Verra VM0033/Plan Vivo etc\. The MRV Framework sets the reporting architecture; this report provides two of its most operationally critical inputs: the AoI boundary definitions that anchor site\-level monitoring, and the continuous satellite detection system that tracks change between measurement cycles\.

These four reports are not parallel workstreams that share a geography by coincidence\. They are integrated evidence, designed from the outset to be read together by carbon verifiers, development partners, and government agencies who need to understand how Ghana's principal blue carbon asset is being measured, tracked, and protected\.

__Chapter 3__

__Free, Prior and Informed Consent — Four AoI Communities__

# Chapter 3: Free, Prior and Informed Consent and Community Engagement

## 3\.1 Why FPIC is Inseparable from Drone Monitoring in This Context

A drone survey over the Keta Lagoon Complex is not a neutral technical exercise conducted above an empty landscape\. It is an aerial survey of community land, land whose management, use, and ownership are structured by a tripartite governance system that has no analogue in most of the contexts where drone monitoring is treated as a routine remote sensing operation\. Authority over the Lagoon's mangrove zones is distributed across customary institutions \(chiefs, queen mothers, clan heads, and elders who hold the social licence to grant or withhold access\), statutory agencies \(the Wildlife Division, District Assemblies holding legal jurisdiction over Ramsar site management\), and the emerging community\-based structures through which the WACA Blue Carbon initiative is being operationalized, i\.e Community Change Agents, Community Resource Management Committees, and the women's groups and VSLAs that represent the most functionally effective governance organisations at the grassroots level\. Any survey drone transiting over the four Areas of Interest that constitute the primary monitoring footprint of this report is flying over land that these institutions govern\.

The data collected by that drone, centimetre\-resolution orthomosaics, Structure from Motion topographic models, multispectral canopy condition imagery will not remain a scientific record\. It will be used to define the spatial boundaries of carbon credit accounting, guided by IPCC standards and site\-specific methodology selection, under Verra VM0033/Plan Vivo, etc or a comparable voluntary market methodology\. 

The Risk Assessment and Safeguards Report, prepared as part of the August 2025 baseline package, identifies FPIC failure as a top\-tier institutional risk: the determination that inadequate consent processes "could jeopardize project funding and the international validation of any carbon credits generated\." That is not a social development caveat appended to a technical report\. It is the project's own diagnosis of the consequences of conducting drone monitoring without documented social licence\. This chapter records what has been done to establish that licence and what remains to be completed\.

## 3\.2 The FPIC Principle in the WACA Context

__Free__ consent, in this project context, requires more than the absence of direct coercion\. It requires structural care about the difference between the consent of institutions and the consent of communities\. Traditional authority figures; chiefs, queen mothers, elders, hold genuine social legitimacy and their engagement is an indispensable precondition for any field activity\. The Governance, Institutions, and CREMA Readiness Report documents a governance system where traditional decision\-making bodies are "almost always male\-led" and where women, youth, migrants, and persons with disabilities have "limited to no representation\." A consent process that routes exclusively through these structures produces documented agreement from gatekeepers without meaningful engagement with the primary users of the land being monitored\. Separate, targeted consultation with women's groups, youth organisations, and migrant populations is not a courtesy addition to the FPIC process, it is a compliance requirement\.

__Prior__ consent means that drone flights are conducted only after community engagement is completed, not in parallel with it or after the fact\. In practice for this workstream, it means that the AoI boundary files were not uploaded to the EOSDA platform for continuous monitoring configuration, and no orthomosaic imagery has been formally disclosed or used in any carbon accounting calculation, before the communities adjacent to each AoI had been engaged about the purpose and implications of that monitoring\. It also means that if EOSDA alert signals from a monitored AoI are to be used in future verification submissions, the FPIC process must have been completed and documented before those submissions are made\. The prior requirement extends across the project lifecycle: communities that consented to baseline mapping activities during the June–August 2025 fieldwork have not thereby consented to the specific boundary configurations of the AoIs established in February–March 2026, to the access restrictions that may be associated with designated restoration zones, or to the terms of any future carbon credit arrangement\. Each of these represents a materially distinct intervention requiring its own documented FPIC process\.

__Informed__ consent in communities where the concept of carbon markets has no established local referent, where carbon rights, credit verification, and benefit\-sharing schedules are not familiar frameworks requires sustained, multilingual, and multi\-format information disclosure\. The Governance Report documents a landscape shaped by "community fatigue from previous, unfulfilled development promises\." In that context, information disclosure that overstates certainties, glosses over timelines, or neglects to explain the conditions under which carbon credits are validated and payments made will generate initial agreement that dissolves into passive non\-compliance when reality fails to match expectation\. Informed consent in this project must communicate honestly: what drones are and what they photograph; who will hold and access the imagery; how monitoring data supports carbon credit claims; what financial benefits communities might receive and over what timeframe; and what restrictions on land use, if any, are associated with monitored zones\. That communication must be delivered in Ewe \(the dominant language of the Keta Lagoon communities\), Fante \(where applicable for adjacent communities\), and through visual formats, participatory discussion, and community radio where literacy barriers are documented\.

## 3\.3 The FPIC Process Conducted for the Drone Survey AoIs

The four Areas of Interest for the drone survey, Aborlove Nolopi, Agbatsivi\-Agortoe, Salo\-Agortoe Stretch, and Anyanui Combine fall within the broader Keta Lagoon landscape where the project has conducted extensive FPIC processes with all communities\. The drone\-specific FPIC documented in this section is focused on the communities that fall within or adjacent to these four AoIs, in line with the specific monitoring boundaries and the direct implications of aerial survey and carbon accounting within those boundaries\. The wider project\-level FPIC register covers all communities in the programme landscape\.

The engagement process supporting the drone survey and EOSDA configuration was initiated as an extension of the community engagement conducted during the June–August 2025 socio\-economic baseline fieldwork, which itself operated under FPIC\-compliant protocols aligned with the project's Environmental and Social Safeguards Framework\. The baseline engagement established the foundational relationships, institutional contacts, and consent documentation framework upon which the drone survey FPIC process has built\. What follows, documents the AoI\-specific engagement activities conducted between the conclusion of the baseline fieldwork and the completion of the AoI boundary datasets in February–March 2026\.

### AoI 1: Aborlove Nolopi \(52 Ha\) \- Eastern Cluster

The Aborlove Nolopi AoI covers a mixed landscape of degraded and recovering mangrove in the eastern sector of the Lagoon\. Community engagement for this AoI was conducted with the Aborlove\-Norlopi traditional council, whose jurisdiction encompasses the land within and adjacent to the monitoring boundary\. The consultation established the foundational social licence for survey operations over this zone, with the project team providing a structured briefing on the purpose of drone overflights, the nature of the orthomosaic and multispectral data to be collected, and the connection between aerial monitoring and the carbon inventory work underway at the adjacent baseline plots\. Community information meetings were facilitated by the CCA assigned to this cluster, who provided Ewe\-language translation and subsequent community follow\-up to ensure that the information presented in the formal meeting was understood at the household level\.

### AoI 2: Agatsivi\-Agortoe \(762 Ha\) \- Central Cluster

The Agbatsivi\-Agortoe AoI is the largest of the four monitoring zones, spanning contiguous and fragmented canopy across a landscape subject to sustained fuelwood pressure\. Multiple community units fall within or adjacent to this AoI: Agortoe and Agbastivi making this the most complex of the four FPIC processes in terms of the number of separate governance structures requiring engagement\.

Traditional authority engagement was conducted sequentially with the Clan Heads, Mangrove Owners and Elders governing each of the three adjacent communities, recognising that a single consolidated meeting convened by one chief would risk the appearance of that chief speaking for communities over whom their jurisdiction is limited or contested\. Separate consultation meetings with each council were documented individually\. The Agbastivi community cluster includes Galotse as an adjacent settlement, the Governance Report identifies Galotse as a community where "Queen Mothers were instrumental in mobilizing female participation" for project activities during the baseline\. 

The multi\-community character of this AoI required that CCA facilitators from each adjacent community participated in the FPIC process, rather than relying on a single CCA whose community affiliation might create perceptions of partiality\. Three CCA facilitators supported the engagement across Galotse, Agortoe, and Agbastivi

### AoI 3: Salo\-Agortoe Stretch \(525 Ha\) \- Central Cluster

The Salo\-Agortoe Stretch encompasses the creek system that is most directly connected to the Salo community's livelihood and land\-use patterns\. The Salo community has an historically intimate relationship with this mangrove zone: the Salo mangrove market operates within or adjacent to this AoI, and many of the Salo Temporary Sample Plots fall within the stretch's monitoring boundary\. That inventory plot is a direct material link between the drone survey AoI and the carbon accounting work to which monitoring outputs will contribute, and it was central to the information disclosed to the Salo community during the FPIC process for this AoI\.

### AoI 4: Anyanui Combine \(128 Ha\) \- Western Cluster

The Anyanui Combine AoI occupies the estuary mouth, where the Keta Lagoon meets tidal marine influence\. Ecosystem health monitoring conducted for the companion Wetland Ecosystem Health Report identified a pH anomaly of 2\.7 at monitoring stations within this AoI a severe acidification signature consistent with tidal blockage by road infrastructure\. The road responsible for that blockage is not an external intrusion into the landscape; it is a community access route serving Anyanui, and Tunu\. Any management recommendation for tidal restoration at this site including the recommendation already documented in the Ecosystem Health Report is simultaneously a governance decision about a piece of infrastructure on which community members depend for daily movement and economic activity\.

The FPIC process for AoI 4 was therefore structured to include explicit disclosure of this connection\. Communities were informed not only about the drone survey and EOSDA monitoring configuration but about the ecosystem health findings that make AoI 4 the AoI most likely to generate a management recommendation in the near term and about the fact that any tidal restoration measure at the estuary mouth would require community consultation and consent on the trade\-offs between hydrological restoration benefits and potential impacts on road access\. 

Traditional authority engagement was conducted with the councils governing Anyanui, and Tunu\. CCA facilitators from each community participated, with particular attention to ensuring that Tunu which is smaller and less institutionally prominent than Anyanui was not treated as appendages of the larger community's consent process\. 

## 3\.4 Community Change Agents as FPIC Facilitators

The Community Change Agents trained during the December 2025 baseline assessment fieldwork served as the primary implementation mechanism for the drone survey FPIC process across all four AoIs\. Their role was not administrative\. It was culturally embedded facilitation: translating technical concepts into accessible community language, situating the drone survey within the broader Blue Carbon initiative that communities had already been introduced to during baseline activities, and providing the social context that determines whether an information meeting produces genuine understanding or performed agreement\.

For the drone survey FPIC process, CCAs played three specific roles\. First, they prepared communities for formal consultation sessions by conducting prior household\-level engagement that allowed community members to arrive at information meetings with foundational awareness rather than encountering the concept of drone monitoring for the first time in a formal setting a practice that substantially improves the quality of consent obtained in the meeting itself\. Second, they provided Ewe\-language facilitation that ensured conceptual accuracy was maintained in translation, rather than the simplification or distortion that occurs when technical concepts are filtered through an interpreter whose accountability is to the external team rather than to the community\. Third, they supported with post\-meeting follow\-up to identify community members who had not attended formal sessions, to ensure that consent documentation did not systematically exclude those whose participation was lowest in formal meeting formats typically women with young children, persons with disabilities, and elderly community members\.

## 3\.5 Inclusive Engagement: Women, Youth, and Marginalized Groups

The central governance finding of the August 2025 baseline across all three Ramsar sites is unambiguous: the primary users of mangrove resources are women who depend on fuelwood for their livelihoods in the fish processing economy and have no formal voice in the development of rules for mangrove management\. This finding is not incidental to the drone survey FPIC process\. Women conducting fuelwood collection traverse the same AoI zones that the drone orthomosaics now document\. Their daily routes, harvesting sites, and use patterns are, in effect, inscribed in the multispectral canopy condition data collected by this survey\. Yet the governance structures through which their communities’ interface with the carbon project traditional councils, interim CRMCs are "almost always male\-led\." A consent process that routes exclusively through those structures will document the consent of the institutions that govern land on paper without the consent of the people who govern it in practice\.

The FPIC process for each AoI therefore required separate, dedicated women's consultation sessions as a non\-negotiable protocol step, not as a supplementary engagement for women willing to attend mixed\-format meetings\. The logistics of those sessions were designed to be accessible: scheduled to avoid the peak hours of fish smoking and market activity, convened at accessible locations within the community rather than at formal meeting halls, and facilitated by female CCAs or female community liaisons where available\. The information package delivered in women's sessions covered the same substantive content as community\-wide meetings, with specific attention to two topics that are most directly relevant to women's livelihoods: the possible future access restrictions on fuelwood collection zones that may follow from restoration planning based on drone survey outputs, and the mechanism through which women's groups would receive a share of any carbon finance benefits specifically the commitment that a defined portion of co\-benefits will be channelled through established VSLAs and fish processor associations rather than aggregated at the CREMA committee level where male gatekeeping risks intercepting the flow\.

## 3\.6 Grievance Redress Mechanism \(GRM\)

The Risk Assessment and Safeguards Report establishes that formal grievance mechanisms are "informal or non\-existent in over 80% of communities" profiled across the three Ramsar sites\. For the drone survey and EOSDA monitoring system, this baseline absence of grievance infrastructure creates a specific and immediate risk: if a community member objects to a drone overflight, raises concerns about who is accessing the imagery, or believes their community was not properly consulted before an AoI boundary was registered in the monitoring platform, there is currently no standardised, accessible pathway through which that concern can be received, documented, and resolved\. The absence of that pathway does not prevent grievances from arising; it redirects them into informal channels where they are less visible, less accountable, and more likely to manifest as passive non\-compliance or active opposition\.

The GRM established under the WACA Blue Carbon initiative, and which applies to the drone survey and EOSDA monitoring workstream, operates through four escalation tiers designed to match the severity and complexity of grievances with the appropriate resolution authority\.

For this drone survey, the CCAs and designated CRMC Grievance Officers served as the accessible community\-level point of contact for any concern arising from drone flight activities\. It was designed to handle the majority of drone and EOSDA monitoring grievances: confusion about what the drone photographed or who has access to the imagery, concerns about whether a particular community was adequately consulted before its AoI was activated, or requests for information about what happens if a change alert is detected\. The CCA network's effectiveness in this role derives from the same quality that makes them effective FPIC facilitators: they are peer\-nominated, locally trusted, and accountable to the community rather than to the project team\. Other tiers of grievance including the CREMA Executive Committee with the participation of traditional leaders, and representatives from the District Assembly and the Wildlife Division were all available to support the process\.

## 3\.7 Data Sovereignty and Community Access to Monitoring Data

Communities have access to community\-level data summaries derived from the orthomosaics: canopy condition maps, extent measurements, and the boundary files that define their AoI within the national monitoring system\. These derived products allow community governance structures CREMA committees, CCAs, traditional councils to verify that the monitoring boundaries registered in the platform correspond to the boundaries discussed and consented to during the FPIC process, and to track the ecosystem condition of their land over time without requiring technical access to the raw orthomosaic files\.

The EOSDA platform dashboard and its configuration for the four Keta Lagoon AoIs is currently accessible at the project team level\. Extending CCA\-level access to the monitoring dashboard allowing designated community monitors to view the NDVI or NDMI time series and alert status for their AoI  is a technical step that has not yet been completed\. This access is not a luxury; it is the operational expression of the principle that local communities are co\-custodians of their own landscape monitoring system\.

__Chapter 4__

__Drone Survey Methodology \- DJI Mavic 3M Platform__

# Chapter 4: Drone Survey Methodology

## 4\.1 The Three\-Output Survey Architecture

A drone survey of the Keta Lagoon Areas of Interest is not, in the first instance, a photography exercise\. It is a multi\-output data collection mission whose three primary products an orthomosaic, a topographic dataset, and a calibrated multispectral reflectance archive  are designed to answer fundamentally different questions about the mangrove ecosystem and its trajectory\. Understanding the survey's methodology requires understanding, at the outset, that all three outputs are produced from a single flight mission using the same drone and the same sensor payload\. 

The first output the orthomosaic is a georeferenced, seamless photomosaic of the entire AoI at 5\-to\-10\-centimetre ground sample distance \(GSD\), assembled by structure\-from\-motion photogrammetry from the raw imagery archive\. As a visual product, the orthomosaic is the aerial equivalent of a satellite true\-colour image, but at a resolution approximately 1,000 to 2,000 times finer than the 10 to 20 metre Sentinel\-2 imagery used by the GIS team to produce the national mangrove extent baseline\. Where the Sentinel\-2 pixel is a single spectral average across a square area that may contain several tree crowns, a dirt path, and a shallow water margin, the orthomosaic resolves individual canopy crowns, canopy gaps, root exposure, and water\-surface texture within the same area\. This resolution difference is not incidental it is the basis of the orthomosaic's primary analytical function: the direct validation and, where necessary, supersession of the satellite classification within the four AoIs\.

The second output the topographic map output is a three\-dimensional point cloud and associated terrain model capturing micro\-elevation differences in the range of 5 to 20 centimetres\. These differences are entirely invisible to satellite sensors, which measure spectral reflectance and cannot infer ground elevation directly\. At the scale of a mangrove restoration site, micro\-topographic variation of this magnitude is operationally decisive: a 15\-centimetre elevation difference determines whether a given patch is inundated at mean high tide, which in turn determines whether a newly planted propagule survives its first wet season\. The topographic output is therefore the restoration design tool the dataset that answers questions about drainage channel geometry, tidal connectivity, and micro\-topographic feasibility that no other instrument available to the project can answer at the spatial resolution required\.

The third output; the raw multispectral reflectance data from all five bands of the M3M built\-in multispectral sensor is the species identification and vegetation health index dataset\. Calibrated reflectance values across the Green \(560 nm\), Red \(650 nm\), Red Edge \(730 nm\), and Near\-Infrared \(860 nm\) channels are the inputs for NDVI, NDMI, and Red Edge chlorophyll index calculations that enable discrimination between *Rhizophora racemosa* and *Avicennia germinans* canopy at individual tree\-crown scale, quantification of canopy moisture stress as a precursor to die\-back, and calibration of the EOSDA continuous monitoring system to the specific spectral signature of Keta Lagoon mangroves at baseline condition\.

These three outputs together serve two strategic functions within the broader monitoring architecture\. First, they validate and, within the AoI footprints, supersede the satellite\-derived mangrove classification produced by the GIS team\. Second, they provide the T0 calibration anchor the baseline against which all future monitoring outputs are referenced for the EOSDA continuous monitoring system described in this report\.

## 4\.2 Drone and Sensor Platform

The primary survey platform for this workstream is the DJI Mavic 3 Multispectral \(M3M\), an integrated drone and multispectral sensor system developed specifically for vegetation mapping and precision forestry applications\. The integration of airframe and sensor in a single commercially available platform eliminates the alignment and synchronisation errors that affect multi\-component systems where a third\-party sensor is mounted on a generic drone airframe\. The M3M carries an integrated four\-band multispectral camera array with fixed\-focus lenses centred on the Green \(560 nm\), Red \(650 nm\), Red Edge \(730 nm\), and Near\-Infrared \(860 nm\) wavebands  the same spectral bands provided by the professional multispectral sensors used in professional vegetation mapping, here delivered in a more operationally compact configuration\.

The critical technical feature of the M3M for the purposes of photogrammetric accuracy is its integrated RTK \(Real\-Time Kinematic\) positioning system\. RTK positioning provides centimetre\-level GPS accuracy by receiving real\-time correction signals from a fixed ground station or network, eliminating the positional drift that accumulates in conventional GPS\-guided survey drone\. For a mangrove monitoring programme that requires direct comparison between orthomosaics produced in different survey epochs comparing T0 against T1, T1 against T2  the absolute positional accuracy of each mission is as important as its internal resolution\. A 10\-centimetre positional error between two survey epochs, which would be invisible in the 10\-metre Sentinel\-2 data and entirely irrelevant to the national classification workflow, can produce false change signals at 5 to 10 centimetre GSD orthomosaic resolution\. The RTK system, combined with the ground control point network described in Section 4\.3, prevents positional drift from being misread as ecological change\.

Survey operations across the four AoIs were conducted at a flight altitude of 100 to 120 metres above ground level \(AGL\), producing the 5\-to\-10\-centimetre GSD that the monitoring design requires\. Flight plans were configured with 80 percent forward image overlap and 75 percent side overlap parameters that provide the redundant image coverage necessary for robust structure\-from\-motion reconstruction and minimise the risk of data gaps in the photogrammetric model\. The total surveyed area across all four AoIs is 1,467 hectares, distributed as follows: Aborlove Nolopi \(52 Ha, eastern cluster\), Agatsivi\-Agortoe \(762 Ha, central cluster\), Salo\-Agortoe Stretch \(525 Ha, central cluster including Salo Temporary Sample Plots \), and Anyanui Combine \(128 Ha, western cluster\)\. Each AoI was planned and executed as a separate flight mission, with dedicated pre\-flight calibration, ground control point occupation, and post\-flight quality assessment\.

The integrated downwelling light sensor \(DLS 2\) mounted on the top surface of the drone records solar irradiance continuously throughout each flight mission\. This measurement is required for accurate radiometric calibration of the multispectral imagery: because solar irradiance varies with cloud cover, atmospheric conditions, and sun angle during the mission, calibrating pixel reflectance values against an irradiance record rather than assuming constant illumination  is the difference between a dataset that is scientifically comparable across time and one that reflects the weather conditions on a particular morning\.

## 4\.3 Ground Control Point Design and RTK Correction

Ground Control Points \(GCPs\) are the physical anchors that tie the photogrammetric model to real\-world geographic coordinates\. A minimum of five GCPs per AoI were established at the time of survey, with points distributed across the AoI extent to provide even spatial coverage of the model constraint\. Each GCP consists of a highly visible physical marker typically a checkerboard panel or painted target positioned at a location whose coordinates have been measured by RTK GPS to centimetre\-level accuracy\. The GCP coordinates are introduced into the photogrammetric processing workflow as control constraints, forcing the reconstructed three\-dimensional model to conform to real\-world geometry rather than floating in a self\-consistent but positionally arbitrary reference frame\.

For continuous monitoring purposes, the permanence and resolvability of the GCP network across survey epochs is as important as its initial accuracy\. The same GCPs will be reoccupied in every future drone revisit of each AoI  whether that revisit is part of the annual resurvey schedule or triggered by an EOSDA anomaly alert\. By reoccupying the same control network, each future orthomosaic is constrained to the same real\-world coordinate system as the T0 baseline, enabling direct pixel\-to\-pixel comparison between missions without the systematic positional offsets that would otherwise accumulate between surveys conducted months or years apart\. This is not a photogrammetric formality\. The ability to attribute spectral changes in a future orthomosaic to genuine ecological change rather than to differential registration error depends entirely on the consistency of the control network across survey epochs\.

The RTK measurements for GCP positions were carried out using a survey\-grade receiver in conjunction with a network RTK correction service, a co\-located base station, achieving horizontal accuracies of better than 2 centimetres and vertical accuracies of better than 3 centimetres\. These accuracy levels are consistent with the requirements of Verra VM0033 for spatial data used in carbon stock boundary delineation and with the World Bank Environmental and Social Framework's standards for georeferenced community asset mapping\.

## 4\.4 Primary Output 1 \- Orthomosaic: Validation of the Satellite Classification

The orthomosaic is the output that connects the drone survey most directly to the existing scientific record for the Keta Lagoon\. The geo\-social mangrove extent baseline report, produced by the project's GIS team in 2025 using 2024 data, classified 20,717\.95 hectares of mangroves within the Keta Lagoon Complex using Harmonised Sentinel\-2 multispectral imagery at 10 to 20 metre resolution, with a Random Forest classifier trained and validated on 319 reference data points\. That report is explicit about the capabilities and the limits of this approach\. In the section describing the methodological rationale for Sentinel\-2 over earlier Landsat data, the report states that in a heterogeneous landscape like Ghana's coast, where mangroves often exist as narrow fringes along lagoons or in small, fragmented patches, the higher resolution of Sentinel\-2 is critical  it allows for the detection and accurate classification of smaller stands that would have been missed or misclassified as mixed pixels in the coarser 30\-metre Landsat data\. This candid acknowledgement that Sentinel\-2, for all its improvement over Landsat, still generates mixed\-pixel classification uncertainty in fragmented mangrove landscapes defines the validation function that the drone orthomosaic performs\.

The 102\.8 MB keta\_lagoon\.tif classification raster archived in the project repository is the spatial output of that Sentinel\-2 Random Forest analysis\. It is the master spatial reference layer against which the drone orthomosaics are overlaid within the four AoIs\. The validation workflow compares the satellite\-derived binary classification mangrove versus non\-mangrove  at each location within the AoI footprints against the orthomosaic's centimetre\-resolution visual evidence of what is actually present at that location\. This comparison identifies four categories of classification discrepancy that the satellite workflow cannot self\-diagnose\.

Commission errors are locations the Random Forest model classified as mangrove that the orthomosaic reveals are not mangrove\. In a fragmented coastal landscape like the Keta system, these can arise from spectral confusion between mangrove canopy and other dark, dense vegetation types, from water\-shadow effects in pixels straddling open\-water and canopy boundaries, or from the averaging of a mixed\-canopy pixel whose reflectance falls within the training\-data envelope for mangrove even though the actual ground cover is a mosaic of mangrove and mudflat\. At 10 to 20 metres, the classifier makes a binary decision about a pixel whose area might contain only 40 percent mangrove canopy\. At 5\-to\-10\-centimeter GSD, the orthomosaic shows exactly what is inside that pixel and the verification is immediate and unambiguous\.

Omission errors are small mangrove stands fragments smaller than one Sentinel\-2 pixel, that is, smaller than 100 to 400 square metres that the satellite classification missed entirely but that are visible in the orthomosaic\. In the Keta landscape, where the socio\-economic baseline documents the progressive fragmentation of mangrove stands under fuelwood pressure from adjacent fish\-smoking communities, these small fragments represent a significant category of unmapped carbon stock\. The geo\-social report itself identified previously undocumented mangrove stands through participatory resource mapping with community members, and some of these stands may fall below the satellite detection threshold\. The orthomosaic provides the definitive answer\.

Canopy density is a third dimension of validation that the satellite cannot supply directly\. The NDVI derived from Sentinel\-2 provides a proxy for vegetation vigour, but it cannot distinguish a closed\-canopy stand with high leaf area index from an open\-canopy stand with patchy regeneration both can produce similar composite NDVI values at 10\-metre resolution\. The orthomosaic delivers a direct canopy cover percentage per hectare, enabling stratification of the classified mangrove area into condition classes that inform carbon stock estimation and restoration prioritisation\.

Degradation mapping is the fourth and arguably most operationally critical validation function\. Early\-stage thinning the removal of individual trees for fuelwood, producing scattered canopy gaps not yet large enough to register as non\-mangrove at satellite resolution  is the leading edge of the deforestation process documented in the geo\-social baseline\. At 5\-to\-10\-centimetre GSD, canopy gaps produced by individual tree removal are directly visible\. Within the Agbatsivi\-Agortoe AoI, which the monitoring plan identifies as subject to sustained fuelwood pressure from adjacent fish\-smoking operations, detection of early\-stage thinning events between survey epochs is the primary early\-warning function of the orthomosaic monitoring workflow\.

Where the orthomosaic reveals significant discrepancies with the satellite classification commission errors that inflate the classified mangrove area, or omission errors and gap patterns that reveal structural degradation invisible to the satellite the drone\-derived interpretation supersedes the Sentinel\-2 result within the AoI footprints\. For the purposes of carbon accounting under Verra VM0033, the higher\-resolution direct observation takes precedence over the modelled classification\.

## 4\.5 Primary Output 2 \- Topographic Map: Elevation, Drainage, and Restoration Design

The topographic map produced from the structure\-from\-motion photogrammetric processing of the raw imagery is a product that has no equivalent in the satellite toolkit\. No freely available satellite product can resolve micro\-elevation differences in the 5\-to\-20\-centimetre range relevant to mangrove hydrology\. LiDAR surveys can produce comparable vertical precision, but LiDAR campaign costs and logistical requirements are prohibitive at the site scale and operational tempo required for this monitoring programme\. The drone\-derived topographic dataset is therefore a unique and irreplaceable output for restoration planning purposes\.

The ecological rationale for topographic precision is straightforward\. In mangrove ecosystems, tidal inundation frequency the primary driver of species zonation, seedling recruitment success, soil redox conditions, and ultimately carbon accumulation rate is determined by elevation relative to tidal datums\. An area lying 15 centimetres above mean high water experiences a fundamentally different inundation regime from an area lying 15 centimetres below it, even though the two locations may be separated horizontally by only a few metres\. A restoration planting design based on topographic data at satellite resolution which offers no useful elevation information at these scales is equivalent to planting without any consideration of hydrology\. Most propagule mortality in mangrove restoration projects is attributable to incorrect hydrological positioning: propagules planted in areas that are either too frequently inundated for their stage of development or too rarely inundated to sustain their physiological requirements\.

For the Aborlove Nolopi and Anyanui Combine AoIs specifically, the topographic dataset answers a suite of restoration design questions that cannot be addressed by any other instrument available to the project\. Within Anyanui Combine, the ecosystem health baseline has recorded soil pH values in the range of 2\.7 at certain measurement stations an extreme acidity signal that is consistent with oxidation of pyrite\-rich sediments exposed by disrupted tidal flow\. The causal diagnosis whether the acidity results from a natural sediment chemistry anomaly, from the blockage of tidal channels by sedimentation or human disturbance, or from historical pond construction  requires an understanding of the micro\-topographic drainage network that only the topographic map can provide\. Where are the natural drainage channels relative to the pH anomaly locations? Is there a topographic barrier impeding tidal flushing that would otherwise dilute and buffer the acidified soil water? Can tidal reconnection be achieved by the low\-intervention removal of a sediment berm, or does the elevation mismatch between the drainage channel and the target restoration area require more substantial hydrological engineering? The topographic map answers these questions spatially and precisely, enabling the restoration design to be evidence\-based rather than speculative\. Preliminary evidence from the topographic data and community consultations indicates that the primary hydrological challenges at this site are siltation and hampered tidal flow, both of which constrain the feasibility of passive mangrove restoration\.

For Aborlove Nolopi, where the orthomosaic identifies the boundary between recoverable and non\-recoverable canopy as the central restoration question, the topographic data provides the hydrological context needed to interpret that boundary\. Areas where canopy thinning is associated with low\-lying, frequently inundated terrain may be naturally self\-recovering once the pressure of direct extraction is reduced; areas where thinning corresponds to elevated, rarely inundated terrain may require active restoration to return to productive mangrove condition\.

## 4\.6 Primary Output 3 \- Raw Survey Data for Further Analysis

The third output; the raw multispectral reflectance data from all five bands of the M3M built\-in multispectral sensor  is the species identification and vegetation health index dataset\. The project will archive it and with the appropriate resources, calibrate reflectance values across the Green \(560 nm\), Red \(650 nm\), Red Edge \(730 nm\), and Near\-Infrared \(860 nm\) channels which can be used to derive NDVI, NDMI, and Red Edge chlorophyll index calculations that enable discrimination between *Rhizophora racemosa* and *Avicennia germinans* canopy at individual tree\-crown scale, quantification of canopy moisture stress as a precursor to die\-back, and calibration of the EOSDA continuous monitoring system to the specific spectral signature of Keta Lagoon mangroves at baseline condition\.

## 4\.8 Data Processing Workflow

Raw imagery from the M3M flight missions enters a five\-stage processing chain before survey products are ready for delivery to the MRV platform and EOSDA\.

Stage one is radiometric calibration\. Before each flight mission and after its completion, the calibration reflectance panel a reflectance target with known spectral properties is photographed under the same illumination conditions as the survey\. These calibration images, combined with the continuous downwelling light sensor record from the DLS 2, are used to convert the raw digital number values in each band to absolute surface reflectance values\. This step is mandatory for scientific comparability between survey epochs: without radiometric calibration anchored to a known reflectance reference, the same mangrove stand will produce different digital number values on a sunny day versus a cloudy day, making multi\-temporal spectral change analysis impossible\.

Stage two is structure\-from\-motion photogrammetric processing, carried out in Agisoft Metashape or equivalent photogrammetric software\. The processing workflow aligns overlapping images by identifying common feature points across multiple photographs, reconstructs the three\-dimensional geometry of the surveyed surface, incorporates the GCP coordinates as geometric constraints, and produces a dense three\-dimensional point cloud of the AoI\.

Stage three is topographic map generation\. From the dense point cloud, a topographic contour map is derived showing terrain elevation, drainage channel geometry, and tidal inundation potential\. This output is delivered as a georeferenced map layer compatible with the MRV platform spatial database\. In a dense mangrove canopy, topographic map accuracy depends on the availability of ground\-return points through canopy gaps and root spaces; GCP density and distribution are critical to constraining the model where ground points are sparse\.

Stage four is orthomosaic generation\. The photogrammetric model is used to orthorectify each raw image correcting for camera tilt, lens distortion, and terrain relief and the orthorectified images are mosaicked into the seamless, georeferenced orthomosaic product at the survey's achieved GSD\.

Stage five is validation and output preparation\. The orthomosaic and spectral index products are overlaid against the keta\_lagoon\.tif master raster and the AoI boundary GeoJSON files to produce the validation comparison described in Section 4\.4\. The completed product package orthomosaic, topographic surface model, topographic map, and index rasters for each AoI  is then assembled for upload to the MRV platform data repository and for integration into the EOSDA continuous monitoring configuration\.

__Chapter 5__

__Area of Interest Survey Results \- Spatial Outputs and Condition Baseline__

# Chapter 5: Area of Interest Survey Results \- Spatial Outputs, Condition Mapping, and Monitoring Baseline

## 5\.1 Chapter Purpose and Survey Scope

This chapter presents the Area of Interest \(AoI\)\-level findings from the drone survey campaign conducted across the four priority monitoring zones of the Keta Lagoon Complex\. It is the spatial outputs chapter of this report the record of what the survey found, AoI by AoI, and what those findings mean for the management of Ghana's most significant blue carbon ecosystem\. 

## 5\.2 Aborlove Nolopi \(52 Ha\)

### Pre\-Survey Satellite Classification Baseline

The Aborlove sub\-zone appears in the classification as a mixed mosaic of mangrove canopy and transitional ground cover a pattern consistent with the known pressure dynamics documented in both the geo\-social and ecosystem health baselines\. The classification correctly identifies the presence of mangrove stands in the Aborlove area; what it cannot resolve is the internal structure of those stands: the boundary between closed canopy and canopy in early\-stage decline, the location and extent of regenerating patches, and the presence of canopy gaps too small to influence a 10\-metre satellite pixel but large enough to represent the leading edge of fuelwood extraction pressure\. These are precisely the questions that drive the monitoring design for this AoI\.

### Drone Survey Outputs

The DJI Mavic 3 Multispectral \(M3M\) survey of Aborlove Nolopi was conducted at 100 to 120 metres above ground level with 80 percent forward overlap and 75 percent side overlap, supported by a minimum of five Ground Control Points measured to centimetre accuracy by RTK GPS\. This configuration produced an orthomosaic at 5–10\-centimetre ground sample distance and a photogrammetric topographic surface at matching planimetric resolution, with vertical accuracy in the order of 3–5 centimetres over flat terrain\.

In practice, the Aborlove orthomosaic delivered exactly the level of internal stand detail that the literature and project design had anticipated but that Sentinel\-2 could not provide\. Individual canopy crowns, narrow extraction paths, small canopy gaps, regenerating patches, and transitional zones between closed canopy and exposed substrate are all clearly resolved\. Within the protection\-designated extent, the imagery distinguishes three structural conditions that the satellite classification had aggregated into a single mangrove class: intact, closed canopy; thinned but structurally recoverable canopy with seedlings and saplings present under the remaining crowns; and severely degraded or open patches where mangrove cover has retreated to the margins of the mapped stand\.

These distinctions are not theoretical\. In the northern and eastern portions of the AoI, the orthomosaic shows continuous canopy with only narrow, linear gaps consistent with low\-intensity fuelwood collection that has not yet fragmented the stand\. In the central corridor, a mosaic of small canopy gaps and visible understory regeneration demonstrates that mangrove structure remains recoverable, with seedling clusters and young crowns present within the same tidal envelope\. Along the western and landward margins, by contrast, the imagery confirms that the mangrove fringe has retreated onto a higher, drier substrate now occupied by scrub and bare ground\. This confirms that the “recoverable vs non\-recoverable” canopy boundary in Aborlove can be drawn empirically from the drone data rather than inferred from indirect indicators alone\.

__Orthomosaic \- Aborlove__

__Topographic Map \- Aborlove__

### Topographic Findings and Restoration Implications

The topographic output for Aborlove Nolopi converts these visual distinctions into a hydrological feasibility map for natural regeneration and potential active restoration\. Where the orthomosaic identified thinned or open patches adjacent to intact canopy, the elevation model and derived contour map were used to determine whether those patches lie within the present tidal inundation envelope\. In the central and eastern sectors, degraded patches sit at elevations consistent with regular inundation, with micro\-channels connecting them to the main tidal flow\. In these zones, the combination of visible regeneration in the orthomosaic and confirmed tidal reach in the topographic model indicates that natural recovery is hydrologically feasible if extraction pressure is reduced\.

Along the western and landward edges of the AoI, the topographic surface shows a distinct step\-up in elevation, with degraded patches sitting above the regular tidal range\. Here, the orthomosaic’s absence of seedlings or young crowns is matched by a drier micro\-topographic setting\. Stands that once occupied these positions have effectively exited the mangrove hydrological niche\. The drone\-derived elevation data therefore support a clear management conclusion: these higher, landward patches will not recover to mangrove through protection alone and would require both hydrological intervention and active planting if they are to be brought back into the mangrove system at all\.

Taken together, the orthomosaic and topographic outputs for Aborlove do more than add visual richness to the Sentinel\-2 classification\. They resolve the core question that motivated this AoI’s selection: where, within a protection\-designated but pressured landscape, is the canopy still structurally and hydrologically recoverable, and where has it crossed a threshold into non\-recoverable condition under current tidal dynamics\. For Aborlove Nolopi, the drone survey confirms that this boundary can now be drawn with confidence, providing a concrete basis for zoned protection, targeted enforcement, and prioritisation of any future restoration investment within the AoI\.

## 5\.3 AgBatsivi\-Agortoe \(762 Ha\)

### Pre\-Survey Satellite Classification Baseline

Agatsivi\-Agortoe is the largest AoI in the survey footprint, at 762 hectares covering the central cluster of the Keta Lagoon monitoring architecture\. Within the 2024 Sentinel\-2 classification, this sub\-zone falls within the broader central corridor that the geo\-social baseline identifies as supporting substantial contiguous mangrove coverage the area where large, continuous stretches of *Rhizophora racemosa* are concentrated, and where the dense stand structure produces the high NDVI values that underpin the lagoon's carbon stock estimates\. At the same time, the socio\-economic baseline documents this corridor as the zone of most intense fuelwood extraction pressure: the fish\-smoking communities of Agbatsivi, Galotse, and surrounding settlements draw their primary fuel supply from the mangrove stands in this central landscape, and the GIS analysis identifies the most accessible stands adjacent to major settlements as showing signs of degradation or fragmentation\.

The practical consequence of this tension between the satellite classification's representation of large intact forest and the socio\-economic evidence of intense harvesting pressure is that the Sentinel\-2 classification may present Agatsivi\-Agortoe as more intact than the actual on\-ground condition warrants, for the precise reason that early\-stage thinning operates below the satellite detection threshold\. A 20\-metre pixel containing 40 percent canopy and 60 percent gap may still classify as mangrove in a Random Forest trained on reference points selected from intact canopy locations\. It is for this zone that the degradation\-mapping function of the orthomosaic is most operationally critical\.

### Drone Survey Outputs

The Agatsivi\-Agortoe AoI was flown with the DJI Mavic 3 Multispectral platform using the same high\-overlap survey parameters as Aborlove: 100–120 metres above ground level, 80 percent forward overlap, 75 percent side overlap, and a distributed network of RTK\-measured Ground Control Points\. This configuration produced a 5–10\-centimetre GSD orthomosaic over the 762\-hectare footprint and a dense three\-dimensional point cloud that resolved micro\-elevation differences and canopy structure across the site\.

In this larger and more heavily used landscape, the orthomosaic confirms what the Sentinel\-2 baseline could only suggest in aggregate: that Agatsivi\-Agortoe is not a uniform “mangrove block” under pressure, but a finely patterned mosaic of intact canopy cores, fragmented extraction fronts, access corridors, and incipient regeneration pockets\. Fuelwood extraction paths that were invisible at 10 metres emerge clearly as narrow, repeated cuts penetrating from the settlement\-facing margins into the interior stands\. Small canopy gaps coalesce into distinct thinning fronts in some sub\-zones and remain scattered in others\. The drone imagery therefore turns a general classification of “sustained fuelwood pressure” into a mapped pattern of where that pressure is currently most intense\.

The survey also resolves structural differences within the nominally “intact” portions of the classification\. In several central compartments, crowns are dense and continuous with minimal gap frequency, confirming true closed canopy conditions more consistent with conservation management\. In adjacent strips, canopy appears visually continuous at Sentinel\-2 scale but, at 5–10 centimetres, shows subtle crown thinning, repeated small gaps, and edge fraying consistent with early\-stage degradation\. The orthomosaic therefore answers a key question posed in the project design: whether the largest AoI is best treated as a single homogeneous monitoring unit for fuelwood pressure, or whether internal zoning is justified\. The drone evidence supports the latter position\.

__Orthomosaic \- Agatsivi\-Agortoe__

__Topographic Map \- Agatsivi\-Agortoe__

## 5\.4 Salo\-Agortoe Stretch \(525 Ha\)

### Pre\-Survey Satellite Classification Baseline and Connectivity Context

At 525 hectares, Salo\-Agortoe Stretch is the second\-largest AoI and is positioned as a central cluster unit geographically contiguous with or connected by creek corridor to the Agatsivi\-Agortoe zone\. The Sentinel\-2 classification for the Salo sub\-zone is among the most ecologically significant in the Keta Lagoon, because the ecosystem health baseline identified the Salo and Galotse area as supporting some of the best\-developed *Rhizophora racemosa* stands in the Complex  stands where soil humus reached 40\.6 percent at Galotse and 37\.5 percent at Agortoe, and where salinity values between 10\.6 and 40 ppt are confirmed as supportive of active mangrove growth\. These are the conditions most favourable to the carbon stock accumulation the project is designed to protect\. The satellite classification for this sub\-zone is expected to show high NDVI values consistent with dense, productive canopy but the drone survey provides the structural and spectral resolution that the classification cannot, distinguishing closed\-canopy stands with high leaf area index from open\-canopy patches with comparable composite spectral signature\.

### Drone Survey Outputs\.

In Salo, where the ecosystem health baseline recorded some of the best\-developed Rhizophora racemosa stands in the entire Keta system, the drone imagery confirms that the core stands genuinely match that description\. Within the central Salo–Galotse corridor, the orthomosaic shows tightly packed crowns with very low gap frequency, continuous prop\-root structure along creek edges, and little evidence of recent extraction scars\. This validates the use of this sub\-zone as a carbon inventory anchor area rather than just a high\-NDVI patch on a satellite map\. At the same time, the orthomosaic reveals that this high\-quality core is fringed by narrower bands where canopy openness, small gaps, and visible access traces indicate early\-stage pressure that the Sentinel\-2 classification could not isolate on its own\.

Within the Salo TSP/PSP footprint, canopy structure is nearly continuous and gap\-free; just outside that footprint, small but repeated gaps, crown edge thinning, and occasional bare patches appear along boat access and footpaths\. The orthomosaic therefore answers a key underlying question for this AoI: whether the Salo PSP truly represents the upper end of structural condition in the corridor, or whether it is simply one more typical stand within a uniformly intact system\. The drone data confirm that the PSP area does function as a structural benchmark within a more heterogeneous landscape\.

By contrast, at the outer margins of the Salo\-Agortoe Stretch, the topographic surface shows localised rises where degraded patches lie just above regular tidal reach\. Here, the orthomosaic’s absence of vigorous regeneration is matched by a marginal elevation that reduces inundation frequency\. In these pockets, the drone data support a more cautious conclusion: passive protection and planting alone are unlikely to deliver full recovery without attention to micro\-topographic adjustment or reconnection of blocked channels\. For Salo\-Agortoe Stretch as a whole, the drone survey therefore confirms that the AoI indeed functions as a carbon inventory anchor, but it also delineates where that anchor is strongest, where early\-stage degradation is emerging around its edges, and where hydrologically feasible restoration opportunities are concentrated within the designated restoration polygon\.

__Orthomosaic \- Salo\-Agortoe Stretch__

__Topographic Map \- Salo\-Agortoe Stretch__

## 5\.5 Anyanui Combine \(128 Ha\)

### Pre\-Survey Satellite Classification and Stress Context

Anyanui Combine occupies the western cluster of the four\-AoI monitoring footprint\. At 128 hectares, it is the second\-smallest AoI but the one carrying the most acute ecological stress signal in the dataset\.

Within the Sentinel\-2 classification, the Anyanui sub\-zone presents a landscape of fragmented or transitional mangrove not the dense, contiguous canopy of the Salo\-Galotse corridor, but a mosaic consistent with a stressed and partially degraded system\. Whether the satellite classification accurately characterises the extent of mangrove presence and absence in this zone, or whether mixed\-pixel averaging inflates or deflates the apparent cover, is precisely the question the orthomosaic answers\.

### Drone Survey Outputs: 

In a zone where Sentinel\-2 indicated a fragmented, stressed mangrove system and the ecosystem health baseline recorded an acid sulphate pH anomaly of 2\.7, the drone survey provides the first detailed, stand\-scale picture of what that stress looks like on the ground\.

The orthomosaic confirms that the satellite classification’s depiction of fragmentation is not an artefact of mixed pixels; it is a real structural condition\. Mangrove stands are broken into small, irregular patches separated by expanses of exposed, pale substrate and waterlogged flats, with canopy edges showing clear retreat lines away from the road embankment and other raised features\. In several locations landward of the Anyanui–Tunu road, the imagery shows dead or severely stressed trees, sparse crown cover, and bare, cracked surfaces consistent with oxidised, acidifying sediments\. Conversely, along certain creek lines and lower\-lying pockets closer to the tidal channel, pockets of denser, greener canopy and seedling clusters indicate that not all of the AoI is equally compromised\.

This spatial differentiation is exactly what the literature suggested a drone survey should reveal in an acid sulphate risk zone, and in Anyanui the survey confirms it\. Instead of a uniformly “degraded” label, the orthomosaic separates the AoI into active stress cores, transitional bands, and pockets of residual healthy or recovering mangrove\. That distinction is critical: it identifies where the pH anomaly is associated with visible structural collapse, and where, despite the chemical signal, the stand still retains enough structure and recruitment to respond positively if hydrology is restored\.

### Topographic Diagnostic Function

The topographic model at Anyanui Combine turns a general hypothesis about road\-induced hydrological blockage into a mapped diagnostic\. The elevation surface shows the Anyanui–Tunu road as a raised embankment sitting above the surrounding tidal flat, with clear evidence that natural drainage channels flatten out or terminate against the road alignment on the landward side\. On the seaward side, channels remain connected to the estuary, while landward pockets behind the road sit in shallow basins with limited outlet\. This geometry is consistent with the acid sulphate signal recorded in the ecosystem health baseline: tidal water is impeded from flushing these landward pockets, allowing sediments to dry, oxidise, and generate acidity between inundation events\.

The drone\-derived topography also shows where remedial action would be most effective\. In several locations, narrow low points in the embankment coincide with historic or partially infilled channels, suggesting priority sites for culvert installation or channel re\-excavation\. In these positions, a relatively small intervention could reconnect a disproportionately large landward area to regular tidal exchange\. Combined with the orthomosaic evidence of residual healthy mangrove pockets adjacent to these potential reconnection points, the survey supports a clear, operational conclusion: Anyanui Combine is not a uniformly lost system, but a hydrologically damaged one where carefully located tidal restoration works could stabilise and, in some compartments, reverse the observed structural decline\.

In summary, for Anyanui Combine System, the drone survey delivers exactly the clarification that the Sentinel\-2 baseline and acid sulphate pH anomaly called for\. It confirms that fragmentation and stress are real and spatially structured, identifies the parts of the AoI where stands are most and least compromised, and maps the specific hydrological barriers and reconnection opportunities that any future restoration design will need to address\.

__Orthomosaic \- Anyanui Combine__

__Topographic Map \- Anyanui Combine__

## 5\.6 Synthesis Across AoIs

Across the four Areas of Interest, the drone survey does what Chapter 1 argued high\-resolution monitoring must do: it resolves, at stand scale, the ecological and management questions that Sentinel\-2 can only indicate at broad class level\. In Aborlove Nolopi, it separates recoverable from non\-recoverable canopy under current tidal conditions; in Agbatsivi\-Agortoe, it maps the internal geography of extraction pressure and early\-stage degradation; in Salo\-Agortoe Stretch, it confirms the structural strength of the carbon benchmark corridor while identifying its emerging edge pressures and restoration compartments; and in Anyanui Combine, it links visible stand collapse to specific hydrological blockages and identifies where tidal reconnection could still restore function\.

Taken together, these findings provide the chapter with the closure that satellite\-only assessment cannot provide\. The orthomosaics and drone\-derived elevation models do not simply add imagery to the report; they convert broad ecological hypotheses into operational findings\. Each AoI can now be interpreted not only as a mapped polygon, but as a spatially differentiated management unit with clear internal condition, specific restoration logic, and a defensible role in the wider blue carbon monitoring architecture for the Keta Lagoon Complex

__Chapter 6__

__EOSDA Continuous Monitoring Platform and AoI Management Workflow__

# Chapter 6: EOSDA Continuous Monitoring Platform and AoI Management Workflow

## 6\.1 Why Continuous Monitoring is Required

The drone survey establishes the baseline condition of the four priority Areas of Interest at high spatial resolution, but a baseline is not, by itself, a monitoring system\. Mangrove landscapes such as those of the Keta Lagoon Complex are dynamic\. Canopy condition, vegetation moisture, hydrological connectivity, localized harvesting pressure, and restoration response can all change between one drone survey and the next\. A one\-time orthomosaic records the state of an AoI on the day of survey; it does not show what changed two weeks later, which part of the polygon began to deteriorate gradually, or where a field team should go before the next drone mission\. Continuous monitoring is therefore required to convert the survey outputs into an operational management tool rather than a static archive of baseline evidence\.

That requirement extends beyond the four AoIs covered in this report\. Aborlove Nolopi, Agatsivi\-Agortoe, Salo\-Agortoe Stretch, and Anyanui Combine are the first operational monitoring polygons in the present workflow, but they are not necessarily the last\. Additional polygons may be created later under this project as restoration areas are refined, as new high\-priority sites are identified, or as carbon\-accounting units evolve\. Separate projects may also generate new polygons within the same landscape and require a compatible system for repeated observation\. The monitoring architecture must therefore be scalable\. The EOSDA platform is valuable not only because it can host the current four AoIs, but because it provides a standing environment within which future polygons can also be registered, observed, and managed using the same logic of recurrent monitoring and documented response\.

## 6\.2 What the EOSDA Platform Offers

The EOSDA platform provides the recurrent observation layer that complements the high\-resolution drone baseline established in this report\. The drone survey provides centimetre\-scale orthomosaic detail, topographic interpretation, and a strong baseline record of condition within each AoI\. EOSDA provides temporal frequency\. Using Sentinel\-2 imagery, the platform allows each uploaded polygon to be reviewed on a 3\-to\-5\-day cycle, subject to cloud conditions and image availability, so that vegetation condition can be tracked repeatedly through time rather than only at the moment of drone acquisition\.

In operational terms, the platform offers four immediate advantages\. First, it allows each AoI to exist as an active monitoring polygon rather than only as a GIS boundary file stored offline\. Second, it generates repeated index\-based observations that help show whether a polygon is stable, improving, or deteriorating over time\. Third, it helps identify unusual departures from recent vegetation or moisture conditions, allowing the team to detect areas that may require local verification, field inspection, or targeted drone revisit\. Fourth, because the polygons remain in the system over time, the platform builds an auditable record of continued observation that can support reporting, management decision\-making, and future MRV requirements\. The significance of EOSDA is therefore not only that it displays imagery, but that it sustains a monitoring record around the same AoIs defined by the drone survey and used throughout this report\.

## 6\.3 Analysis That Can Be Conducted with Uploaded AoIs

Once an AoI has been uploaded to EOSDA, the platform supports several forms of analysis relevant to wetland and mangrove monitoring\. The most immediately useful for this workstream are vegetation and moisture\-related indicators, particularly NDVI and NDMI\. NDVI provides a broad indication of vegetation vigour and canopy greenness, helping the monitoring team understand the general condition and trend of vegetation within a polygon over time\. NDMI provides a more moisture\-sensitive signal and is therefore particularly relevant in mangrove systems where hydrological stress, drying, drainage impairment, or early vegetation stress may emerge before obvious canopy loss is visible in standard imagery\.

These indicators support four practical forms of analysis\. The first is trend analysis, through which the Client can assess whether a polygon is broadly stable, recovering, or declining across a sequence of observation dates\. The second is anomaly detection, where unusual departures from the expected spectral behaviour of the polygon may indicate ecological stress, disturbance, or land\-use pressure\. The third is comparative analysis, allowing different polygons to be reviewed side by side to determine which areas are showing the strongest warning signals\. The fourth is verification support, by helping determine whether a suspected change warrants local inquiry, field checking, or drone revisit\. EOSDA does not replace ground verification or future drone surveys; rather, it improves their efficiency by identifying where those more resource\-intensive interventions are most justified\.

## 6\.4 Initial Upload of the Four AoIs and Addition of Future Polygons

The first stage of implementation is the upload of the four current AoIs into the EOSDA platform\. These are Aborlove Nolopi, Agatsivi\-Agortoe, Salo\-Agortoe Stretch, and Anyanui Combine, the same monitoring units defined through the drone survey and used throughout this report\. Their ingestion into EOSDA should be done using the final validated GeoJSON or KML boundary files, with each polygon uploaded separately and retained under the same approved naming convention used in the survey outputs and project documentation\. This naming consistency is important because it ensures that the platform, the drone archive, field notes, and future reporting all refer to the same spatial units without ambiguity\.

Once uploaded, each polygon becomes an active monitoring unit within the platform\. These initial four uploads serve a dual function\. They provide immediate continuous monitoring coverage for the areas assessed under the present consultancy, and they establish the workflow by which further polygons can be added in future\. Any new polygon generated under this project or by a separate but related initiative can be brought into the same monitoring environment provided it has a validated boundary and a defined monitoring purpose\. The process is straightforward: prepare and validate the new boundary file, upload it under an approved name, check its location and integrity, and begin monitoring its time\-series outputs within the same EOSDA account\. In this way, the present configuration should be understood not as a closed setup for only four sites, but as the foundation of an expandable monitoring system\.

## 6\.5 Handover of the EOSDA Platform to the Client

Because the purpose of this workstream is to leave behind a functioning monitoring capability, the EOSDA platform must be handed over to the Client as part of the final outputs\. This handover should include the platform credentials associated with the project account, the names and status of the four uploaded AoIs, and clear guidance on how the Client can log in, view the registered polygons, review the monitoring outputs, and add new polygons going forward\. The transfer of credentials is not an administrative detail\. It is the mechanism through which operational control of the continuous monitoring workflow passes from the consulting team to the Client\.

The handover is also essential for continuity\. A monitoring platform has limited value if it remains dependent on the consultants who configured it\. By transferring account access and ensuring that the four AoIs are already uploaded within the active platform, the Client receives not only the results of the present assignment but the means to continue the monitoring process after the consultancy ends\. The Client is thereby positioned to review recurrent observations, track future changes, register additional polygons, and sustain the monitoring record over time\. In practical terms, the EOSDA account, the uploaded AoIs, and the accompanying usage guidance together constitute a core deliverable of this report and an important part of the project’s operational handover package\.
